title: Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
text: single global λ\lambda fragile: even one item poorly-correlated LLM predictions, ability-risk criterion may force global optimum λ=0\lambda = 0, preventing items benefiting. Per-item λj\lambda_j allows item draw LLM data optimal level. Consider test eight items : - Items 1–4 straightforward factual questions — LLM predicts well. - Items 5–8 require nuanced reasoning — LLM nearly random . scalar λ\lambda, four poor items push optimum toward 0. per-item λj\lambda_j, items 1–4 get λj≈0.5\lambda_j \approx 0.5–0.7 items 5–8 get λj≈0\lambda_j \approx 0.

title: Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
text: first four items perfect paired predictions (F = Y); items 5–8 near-random LLM predictions.
code: library(mixedsubjectsirt) library(ggplot2)  set.seed(2026)  n_human    <- 400 n_generated <- 1200 n_items    <- 8 n_good     <- 4   # items where LLM predicts well  true_pars <- data.frame(   item = paste0("Item", seq_len(n_items)),   a    = seq(0.8, 1.6, length.out = n_items),   d    = seq(-1.1, 1.1, length.out = n_items) ) true_pars$b <- -true_pars$d / true_pars$a  theta_human <- rnorm(n_human) observed    <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, true_pars)  # LLM: good for items 1–4 (same DGP), poor for 5–8 (random noise) llm_pars_good <- true_pars llm_pars_poor <- true_pars llm_pars_poor$a <- pmax(0.05, rnorm(n_items, 0, 0.1))  # near-random llm_pars_poor$d <- rnorm(n_items, 0, 2) llm_pars_poor$b <- -llm_pars_poor$d / llm_pars_poor$a  # Build predicted (same subjects as human) predicted <- observed   # F = Y for first 4 items predicted[, 5:8] <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, llm_pars_poor)[, 5:8]  # Build generated generated_good <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated), true_pars) generated_poor <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated), llm_pars_poor) generated <- cbind(generated_good[, 1:4], generated_poor[, 5:8]) colnames(generated) <- true_pars$item

what: Step 1: Fit 2PL baseline and get global scalar lambda
title: Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
text: global scalar forced compromise — four poor items constrain value smaller items 1–4 support.
code: human_pars <- fit_2pl(observed, technical = list(NCYCLES = 500))$pars  global_tuned <- tune_lambda_ability_risk(   lambda_grid  = seq(0, 1, by = 0.1),   observed     = observed,   predicted    = predicted,   generated    = generated,   initial_pars = human_pars,   fit_fn       = fit_mixed_subjects_mml,   n_quad       = 11,   control      = list(maxit = 200) )  cat("Global scalar best lambda:", global_tuned$best_lambda, "\n") #> Global scalar best lambda: 0.4

what: Step 2: PPI++ score per item (fast diagnostic)
title: Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
text: tune_lambda_ppi_score_item() applies Proposition 2 formula independently per item using 2×2 diagonal block H−1H^{-1} item-level sub-vectors score matrices. fast (fitting required) shows items well-predicted. Items 1–4 (F = Y) show λj≈N/(n+N)=0.75\lambda_j \approx N/(n+N) = 0.75; items 5–8 (random LLM) show λj≈0\lambda_j \approx 0.
code: ppi_item <- tune_lambda_ppi_score_item(   observed    = observed,   predicted   = predicted,   item_pars   = human_pars,   n_generated = n_generated,   n_quad      = 11 )  cat("Per-item PPI++ lambda:\n") #> Per-item PPI++ lambda: print(data.frame(item = ppi_item$item, lambda = round(ppi_item$lambda, 3))) #>    item lambda #> 1 Item1   0.75 #> 2 Item2   0.75 #> 3 Item3   0.75 #> 4 Item4   0.75 #> 5 Item5   0.00 #> 6 Item6   0.00 #> 7 Item7   0.00 #> 8 Item8   0.00 cat("N/(n+N) upper bound:", round(n_generated / (n_human + n_generated), 3), "\n") #> N/(n+N) upper bound: 0.75

title: Per-Item Lambda (Experimental)
text: tune_lambda_ability_risk_item() uses coordinate descent: item jj, finds λj\lambda_j lambda_grid minimises ability-score risk holding λj′\lambda_{j'} fixed. evaluation fits frozen expected-count Q-function (full marginal-MML objective) IRT marginal likelihood decompose item-wise. Starting global scalar optimum (-zeros) essential — see note . Items 1–4 receive positive λj\lambda_j (good predictor); items 5–8 near zero (poor predictor).
code: item_tuned <- tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(   lambda_grid  = seq(0, 1, by = 0.25),   observed     = observed,   predicted    = predicted,   generated    = generated,   initial_pars = human_pars,   init_lambda  = global_tuned$best_lambda,   # start from global best   n_quad       = 11,   n_pass       = 1,   control      = list(maxit = 200) )  cat("Per-item ability-risk lambda:\n") #> Per-item ability-risk lambda: print(data.frame(item = item_tuned$item, lambda = round(item_tuned$lambda, 3))) #>    item lambda #> 1 Item1    0.4 #> 2 Item2    0.4 #> 3 Item3    0.4 #> 4 Item4    0.4 #> 5 Item5    0.0 #> 6 Item6    0.0 #> 7 Item7    0.0 #> 8 Item8    0.0

text: Starting coordinate descent -zeros recommended. items λj=0\lambda_j = 0, single-item improvement diluted across full ability-risk criterion, making improvements hard detect. recommended workflow : Fit scalar global optimum tune_lambda_ability_risk(..., fit_fn = fit_mixed_subjects_mml). Pass global value init_lambda tune_lambda_ability_risk_item(). coordinate descent identifies items deviate global default — raising λj\lambda_j well-predicted items lowering poorly-predicted ones.

Report per-item results approximate / experimental. Use vcov(item_tuned$final_fit) uncertainty (applies vcov_mixed_subjects vector-lambda bread meat scaling).

title: Choosing Lambda in Mixed-Subjects IRT
text: two lambda objectives: tune_lambda_ppi_score() minimises Tr(Σγ)\text{Tr}(\Sigma_\gamma) — trace item-parameter covariance matrix (PPI++ Proposition 2). theoretical diagnostic: measures whether LLM prediction reduces item-parameter variance. answers “predictor statistically useful?” tune_lambda_ability_risk() minimises E[g′Σγg]E[g'\Sigma_\gamma g] — propagated ability-score risk. practical criterion: answers “using LLM improve downstream test scoring?” Use operational calibration. two estimators: original fit_mixed_subjects() uses frozen expected-count posteriors. creates gradient asymmetry LLM item parameters differ human parameters, systematically inflating discriminations driving tune_lambda_ability_risk select λ=0\lambda = 0 even LLM genuinely informative. fit_mixed_subjects_mml() recomputes posteriors every gradient evaluation, eliminating asymmetry. sandwich covariance (vcov_mixed_subjects_mml, called automatically via vcov()) uses Louis’ (1982) observed-information correction bread, giving honest uncertainty estimates. Use MML estimator unless speed binding constraint.

title: Choosing Lambda in Mixed-Subjects IRT
text: examples use human_pars initial_pars speed.
code: library(mixedsubjectsirt)  set.seed(2027)  n_human     <- 120 n_generated <- 350 n_items     <- 5  human_pars <- data.frame(   item = paste0("Item", seq_len(n_items)),   a    = c(0.8, 1.0, 1.2, 1.35, 1.55),   d    = c(-0.8, -0.3, 0.1, 0.45, 0.9) ) human_pars$b <- -human_pars$d / human_pars$a  llm_pars   <- human_pars llm_pars$a <- pmax(0.35, 0.9 * human_pars$a) llm_pars$d <- human_pars$d + 0.25 llm_pars$b <- -llm_pars$d / llm_pars$a  theta_human <- rnorm(n_human) observed    <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, human_pars) predicted   <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, llm_pars) generated   <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated), llm_pars)  lambda_grid <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1)

MML estimator removes asymmetry. good predictor (F = Y), tune_lambda_ability_risk MML correctly selects λ>0\lambda > 0. selection_risk Inf candidate non-zero convergence code non-finite risk, protecting selection numerical failures. selected calibration carries correctly-sized Louis-corrected covariance:

title: IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
text: document diagnostic guide frozen expected-count estimator (fit_mixed_subjects). explains gradient asymmetry occurs LLM item parameters differ human parameters evaluates IRT scale-linking methods partial remedy. recommended workflow, see Mixed-Subjects IRT Calibration vignette, uses fit_mixed_subjects_mml() — marginal-MML estimator eliminates gradient asymmetry without requiring linking pre-processing step.

title: IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
text: key E-step decision frozen expected-count calibration item parameters use computing posterior quadrature weights LLM-generated responses. frozen estimator uses human-calibrated parameters three datasets (observed, predicted, generated), misspecified LLM different item parameters. misspecification creates systematic gradient asymmetry combined objective — correction gradient ∇(Lgen−Lpred)\nabla(L_\text{gen} - L_\text{pred}) consistently larger zero LLM-specific posteriors diffuse human posteriors. result false minimum combined loss unrealistically large discrimination values. standard remedy IRT scale linking: fit 2PL model generated data, transform LLM parameters onto human metric, use linked parameters generated E-step. document evaluates three classical linking methods: characterise well method aligns LLM parameters human scale — crucially — residual misalignment interacts power-tuning parameter λ\lambda.

title: IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
text: transformation θhuman=AθLLM+B\theta_\text{human} = \,\theta_\text{LLM} + B implies †=/Aa^\dagger = /b†=Ab+Bb^\dagger = Ab + B (equivalently d†=d−(/)Bd^\dagger = d - (/)B).
code: library(mixedsubjectsirt) library(ggplot2)  # Apply (A, B) and return a standardised data frame of item parameters. # Guards against degenerate linking constants (Inf, NaN, non-positive A) that # can arise from unusual mirt fits on different platforms. apply_link <- function(source, A, B, slope_lower = 1e-4) {   if (!is.finite(A) || A <= 0 || !is.finite(B)) {     # Degenerate constants: fall back to identity transform     A <- 1     B <- 0   }   pars <- data.frame(     item = source$item,     a    = pmax(slope_lower, source$a / A),     b    = A * source$b + B,     stringsAsFactors = FALSE   )   # Guard b and d against any residual non-finite values   pars$b <- ifelse(is.finite(pars$b), pars$b, 0)   pars$d <- -pars$a * pars$b   list(pars = pars, A = A, B = B) }  link_mean_mean <- function(source, target) {   A <- mean(source$a) / mean(target$a)   B <- mean(target$b) - A * mean(source$b)   apply_link(source, A, B) }  link_mean_sigma <- function(source, target) {   sd_src <- sd(source$b)   # If source difficulties have no variance (degenerate mirt fit), fall back   # to mean-mean linking which does not depend on sd(b).   if (!is.finite(sd_src) || sd_src < 1e-6) {     return(link_mean_mean(source, target))   }   A <- sd(target$b) / sd_src   B <- mean(target$b) - A * mean(source$b)   apply_link(source, A, B) }  # Stocking-Lord with bounded optimisation to prevent item-level overcorrection. # A is constrained to [0.4, 2.5]; large-variance items can otherwise receive # inflated linked discriminations that destabilise the downstream M-step. link_stocking_lord <- function(source, target,                                 theta_grid = seq(-4, 4, by = 0.05),                                 A_bounds   = c(0.4, 2.5),                                 B_bounds   = c(-4,  4)) {   w <- dnorm(theta_grid) / sum(dnorm(theta_grid))    tcc <- function(pars, theta) {     eta <- outer(theta, pars$a, `*`) +       matrix(pars$d, nrow = length(theta), ncol = nrow(pars), byrow = TRUE)     rowSums(plogis(eta))   }    tcc_target <- tcc(target, theta_grid)    criterion <- function(params) {     A <- exp(params[1])     B <- params[2]     sum(w * (tcc_target - tcc(apply_link(source, A, B)$pars, theta_grid))^2)   }    mm   <- link_mean_mean(source, target)   init <- c(log(mm$A), mm$B)    # L-BFGS-B on log(A) keeps A > 0 and enforces the stated bounds   opt <- optim(     par     = init,     fn      = criterion,     method  = "L-BFGS-B",     lower   = c(log(A_bounds[1]), B_bounds[1]),     upper   = c(log(A_bounds[2]), B_bounds[2]),     control = list(factr = 1e-14, maxit = 1000)   )    apply_link(source, exp(opt$par[1]), opt$par[2]) }  rmse <- function(x, y) sqrt(mean((x - y)^2))

title: IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
text: matched sample draws LLM subjects N(0,1) ability distribution humans. shifted sample uses mean = 0.2, SD = 0.9, representing higher-ability, lower-variance LLM response pattern.
code: n_human     <- 400 n_generated <- 1200 n_items     <- 8  true_pars <- data.frame(   item = paste0("Item", seq_len(n_items)),   a    = seq(0.8, 1.6, length.out = n_items),   d    = seq(-1.1, 1.1, length.out = n_items) ) true_pars$b <- -true_pars$d / true_pars$a  # Human responses theta_human <- rnorm(n_human) observed    <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, true_pars)  # LLM: ~10% attenuated discrimination per item, +0.25 logit intercept shift llm_pars_true <- true_pars llm_pars_true$a <- pmax(0.4, 0.9 * true_pars$a + rnorm(n_items, 0, 0.05)) llm_pars_true$d <- true_pars$d + 0.25 + rnorm(n_items, 0, 0.15) llm_pars_true$b <- -llm_pars_true$d / llm_pars_true$a  # Paired predictions and two generated datasets predicted         <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, llm_pars_true) generated_matched <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated),                    llm_pars_true) generated_shifted <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated, mean = 0.2, sd = 0.9), llm_pars_true)

code: theta_seq <- seq(-4, 4, by = 0.1) tcc_fn <- function(pars, theta) {   eta <- outer(theta, pars$a, `*`) +     matrix(pars$d, nrow = length(theta), ncol = nrow(pars), byrow = TRUE)   rowSums(plogis(eta)) }  # Only show matched case for brevity tcc_df <- rbind(   data.frame(theta = theta_seq, tcc = tcc_fn(human_pars, theta_seq),              method = "Human MLE"),   data.frame(theta = theta_seq, tcc = tcc_fn(llm_raw_matched, theta_seq),              method = "LLM unlinked"),   do.call(rbind, lapply(methods, function(m) {     data.frame(theta  = theta_seq,                tcc    = tcc_fn(all_links$matched[[m]]$pars, theta_seq),                method = m)   })) ) tcc_df$method_f <- factor(tcc_df$method,   levels = c("Human MLE","LLM unlinked","mean_mean","mean_sigma","stocking_lord"),   labels = c("Human MLE","LLM unlinked","Mean-mean","Mean-sigma","Stocking-Lord"))  ggplot(tcc_df, aes(theta, tcc, colour = method_f, linewidth = method_f,                    linetype = method_f)) +   geom_line() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c(     "Human MLE"="black","LLM unlinked"="grey60",     "Mean-mean"="#E41A1C","Mean-sigma"="#377EB8","Stocking-Lord"="#4DAF4A")) +   scale_linewidth_manual(     values = c("Human MLE"=1.0,"LLM unlinked"=0.6,                "Mean-mean"=0.8,"Mean-sigma"=0.8,"Stocking-Lord"=0.8)) +   scale_linetype_manual(     values = c("Human MLE"="solid","LLM unlinked"="dashed",                "Mean-mean"="solid","Mean-sigma"="solid","Stocking-Lord"="solid")) +   labs(x = "Ability (θ)", y = "Expected score",        title = "Test characteristic curves — matched ability distribution",        colour = NULL, linewidth = NULL, linetype = NULL) +   theme_minimal(base_size = 11) + theme(legend.position = "bottom")

what: Lambda sweep: how λ\lambda interacts with linking quality
title: IRT Linking and Gradient Asymmetry: Diagnostic Guide
text: Since linking reduces eliminate gradient asymmetry, optimal λ\lambda scenario much smaller 0.5. sweep λ\lambda 0 0.5 characterise tradeoff using LLM data inflating discrimination estimates.  Parameter recovery selected λ values — matched ability distribution plot reveals three regimes: λ≈0\lambda \approx 0 (human-): linking methods give human-estimate, target behaviour LLM alignment uncertain. Small λ\lambda (0.05–0.15): linking methods add modest noise stay close human-estimate. Linked methods begin diverge . Large λ\lambda (≥0.3\geq 0.3): unlinked estimates diverge severely; linked methods also degrade, rate determined residual gradient asymmetry. Linking flattens RMSE-vs-λ\lambda curve, allowing somewhat higher λ\lambda degradation becomes severe. practical benefit: power-tuning range extended.
code: lambda_grid <- c(0, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50)  sweep_lambda <- function(gen_data, linked_pars) {   # Validate linked_pars: if any parameter is non-finite (can happen when   # sd(b) ~ 0 on some platforms and apply_link fallback wasn't triggered),   # substitute human_pars so counts are always valid.   if (!all(is.finite(c(linked_pars$a, linked_pars$d)))) {     linked_pars <- human_pars   }   q_gen <- mixedsubjectsirt:::build_quadrature_summary(gen_data, linked_pars, quad)   lapply(lambda_grid, function(lam) {     fit <- tryCatch(       mixedsubjectsirt:::fit_from_counts(         q_obs$counts, q_pred$counts, q_gen$counts,         initial_pars = human_pars, lambda = lam, control = list(maxit = 500)),       error = function(e) list(         item_pars = data.frame(item = human_pars$item,                                a = rep(NA_real_, nrow(human_pars)),                                b = rep(NA_real_, nrow(human_pars)),                                d = rep(NA_real_, nrow(human_pars))),         value = NA_real_, convergence = 99L)     )     data.frame(       lambda  = lam,       rmse_a  = if (anyNA(fit$item_pars$a)) NA_real_ else rmse(fit$item_pars$a, true_pars$a),       rmse_d  = if (anyNA(fit$item_pars$d)) NA_real_ else rmse(fit$item_pars$d, true_pars$d),       max_a   = if (anyNA(fit$item_pars$a)) NA_real_ else max(fit$item_pars$a),       conv    = fit$convergence     )   }) }  sweep_results <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c("matched","shifted"), function(case) {   gen_data <- if (case == "matched") generated_matched else generated_shifted   do.call(rbind, lapply(c("unlinked", methods), function(m) {     lp <- if (m == "unlinked") human_pars else all_links[[case]][[m]]$pars     rows <- do.call(rbind, sweep_lambda(gen_data, lp))     rows$method <- m     rows$case   <- case     rows   })) }))  sweep_results$method_f <- factor(sweep_results$method,   levels = c("unlinked","mean_mean","mean_sigma","stocking_lord"),   labels = c("Unlinked","Mean-mean","Mean-sigma","Stocking-Lord"))

code: # Recommended workflow: mean-sigma linking for the generated E-step, # human parameters for observed and predicted E-steps, then power-tune lambda. ms_linked_pars <- all_links$matched$mean_sigma$pars  # Build the three quadrature summaries with the correct parameterisation for each. # q_obs and q_pred use human parameters; q_gen uses the linked LLM parameters. q_gen_linked <- mixedsubjectsirt:::build_quadrature_summary(   generated_matched, ms_linked_pars, quad)  risk_tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(c(0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.20, 0.30, 0.50), function(lam) {   fit_counts <- tryCatch(     mixedsubjectsirt:::fit_from_counts(       q_obs$counts, q_pred$counts, q_gen_linked$counts,       initial_pars = human_pars, lambda = lam,       slope_upper = 4,                # prevents divergence at large lambda       control = list(maxit = 500)),     error = function(e) list(item_pars = data.frame(a = rep(NA_real_, n_items),                                                      d = rep(NA_real_, n_items)))   )    # fit_mixed_subjects is used for vcov; ms_linked_pars for all three E-steps   # is a proxy — the risk trend is the quantity of interest.   fit_for_vcov <- tryCatch(     fit_mixed_subjects(       observed = observed, predicted = predicted, generated = generated_matched,       lambda = lam, initial_pars = ms_linked_pars,       n_quad = n_quad, slope_upper = 4, control = list(maxit = 200)),     error = function(e) NULL   )    rmse_a <- if (anyNA(fit_counts$item_pars$a)) NA_real_ else     round(rmse(fit_counts$item_pars$a, true_pars$a), 4)    if (is.null(fit_for_vcov)) {     return(data.frame(lambda = lam, rmse_a = rmse_a, mean_param_var = NA_real_))   }    tryCatch({     Sigma <- vcov_mixed_subjects(fit_for_vcov)     risk  <- ability_risk(observed, fit_for_vcov, vcov = Sigma)     data.frame(lambda = lam, rmse_a = rmse_a,                mean_param_var = round(risk$summary$mean_param_var, 6))   }, error = function(e) {     data.frame(lambda = lam, rmse_a = rmse_a, mean_param_var = NA_real_)   }) }))  knitr::kable(risk_tab, row.names = FALSE,   col.names = c("λ", "RMSE(a)", "Mean ability-score risk"),   caption   = "Ability-score risk and parameter recovery — mean-sigma linking, matched case")

title: Mixed-Subjects IRT Calibration
code: library(mixedsubjectsirt) library(ggplot2)  set.seed(2026)  n_human    <- 400 n_generated <- 1200 n_items    <- 8  true_pars <- data.frame(   item = paste0("Item", seq_len(n_items)),   a    = seq(0.8, 1.6, length.out = n_items),   d    = seq(-1.1, 1.1, length.out = n_items) ) true_pars$b <- -true_pars$d / true_pars$a  theta_human <- rnorm(n_human) observed    <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, true_pars)  # LLM with ~10% attenuated discrimination and a small intercept shift llm_pars <- true_pars llm_pars$a <- pmax(0.4, 0.9 * true_pars$a + rnorm(n_items, 0, 0.05)) llm_pars$d <- true_pars$d + 0.25 + rnorm(n_items, 0, 0.15) llm_pars$b <- -llm_pars$d / llm_pars$a  predicted <- simulate_2pl(theta_human, llm_pars) generated <- simulate_2pl(rnorm(n_generated), llm_pars)

title: Mixed-Subjects IRT Calibration
text: full simulation study confirms recommended workflow behaves intended: λ selection tracks predictor quality — perfect paired predictor (F = Y) selects λ ≈ N/(n+N) = 0.75; useless predictor selects λ ≈ 0. Louis-corrected standard errors honest — vcov() scalar MML fit attains nominal Wald-interval coverage discriminations intercepts, whereas uncorrected EM-Hessian covariance -covers. harm — tuned ability-score RMSE never worse human-calibration; predictor uninformative method falls back . title: Simulation Validation of the Mixed-Subjects MML Estimator
text: regime tune λ ability-score risk record selected value. also report theoretical PPI++-score λ reference. Selected lambda regime (ability-risk tuning, 100 reps). Findings. R1 perfect → λ = 0.750, exactly N / (n + N) = 1200 / 1600, theoretical maximum perfect paired predictor. PPI++-score λ agrees decimal. R4 independent noise → λ ≈ 0 (74% reps select 0): useless predictor correctly rejected. R3 -DGP R5 LLM shift → λ ≈ 0.10: fresh real responses (R3) correlated--biased LLM (R5) carry little signal, criterion uses little . R2 conditional mean → λ ≈ 0 (96% reps select 0). one counterintuitive result discussed next.

title: Simulation Validation of the Mixed-Subjects MML Estimator
text: R2 feeds method exact true probability correct response person — seemingly best possible predictor. Yet λ almost always 0. reason: PPI++ extracts information residuals actual answers model’s expectation. real 0/1 response carries residual; conditional-mean prediction expectation, residual zero carries nothing correct . noisier predictor emits actual responses (R3) therefore useful perfect one emits smooth probabilities (R2). practical lesson applications: benefit synthetic/LLM data, produce sampled responses, averaged probabilities.

what: Scenario 2: Louis-corrected SE coverage (200 reps) — the key check
title: Simulation Validation of the Mixed-Subjects MML Estimator
text: fix λ = 0.5 two regimes combined estimator consistent true parameters (R1 R3), compare empirical coverage Wald intervals built two covariance estimates fit: Louis-corrected marginal sandwich (vcov() dispatch) EM complete-data Hessian bread (vcov_mixed_subjects(), deliberate bypass). Item-parameter CI coverage (200 reps). Nominal targets 0.90 0.95. Louis correction restores nominal coverage; EM bread -covers. Louis intervals attain 0.909/0.955 0.916/0.957 nominal 0.90/0.95, EM bread covers ~0.71/0.79 — understates uncertainty ignores missing information subject’s latent ability. Louis standard errors ~1.6× wider, difference honest interval overconfident one. Coverage broken parameter type confirms correction works uniformly: Coverage parameter type (averaged R1 R3).

Every discrimination package biased downward ~1/√2 ≈ 0.71. bug invisible relative checks (λ-selection), gradient finite-difference checks (loss gradient mutually consistent), standard-error calibration (SEs correctly described biased estimator). coverage ground truth exposed . fix, discrimination coverage rose 0.04 0.91. strongest argument ground-truth simulation validation: catches errors internal consistency checks .

text: score held-sample 1000 subjects known ability compare ability-score RMSE human-calibration (λ = 0) tuned MML calibration. Downstream ability-score RMSE (100 reps). Findings. harm anywhere. tuned RMSE never worse human-(delta ≤ 0 every regime). predictor useless (R2, R4) method falls back human-estimate; helps (R1, R3, R5) improves, modestly. Discrimination unbiased selected λ (|bias_a| ≤ 0.04 everywhere), confirming quadrature fix tuner selecting degenerate fits. RMSE gains small 8-item test measurement-limited: ability scoring dominated irreducible measurement error short test, even perfect predictor (R1) moves scoring RMSE 1.4961 1.4921. method’s larger payoff item-parameter precision — visible Scenario 2 standard errors — matters calibration uncertainty propagates operational use.

previous_headings: Scenario 3: downstream payoff and no-harm (100 reps)
title: Simulation Validation of the Mixed-Subjects MML Estimator
text: Building scenario surfaced failure mode: fractional R2 predictor large λ, MML objective “converged” minimum runaway discrimination (≈ 1000). fit reports huge item information, collapses covariance makes model-based ability risk spuriously small — tuner select even though scores worse held-data. ability-risk tuners now reject candidate whose maximum discrimination exceeds max_discrimination (default 10); 2PL discrimination never real calibration. guard, R2 reps correctly select λ = 0.

title: Mixed-subjects objective function — mixed_subjects_loss

title: Mixed-subjects objective function — mixed_subjects_loss
text: pars Numeric vector item parameters: discriminations followed intercepts d. q_observed Quadrature summary observed human responses, usually returned mixed_subjects_quadrature(). q_predicted Quadrature summary LLM responses/predictions labeled human subjects. q_llm Quadrature summary generated unlabeled LLM responses. lambda Power-tuning parameter [0, 1].

title: Mixed-subjects objective function — mixed_subjects_loss
text: scalar loss.

title: Mixed-subjects objective function — mixed_subjects_loss
text: objective L_observed(pars) + lambda * (L_generated(pars) - L_predicted(pars)). Setting lambda = 0 gives human-expected-count objective.

title: Mixed-subjects objective function — mixed_subjects_loss
